nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish tributaries, small rivers, and swampy zones with dense vegetation and leaf litter in blackwater and clearwater systems

decorPreferences: heavy planting with floating plants to diffuse light
dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and leaf litter to tannin-stain the water

diet.notes: The Hockeystick Pencilfish is a micropredator that accepts micro-flake, crushed dried foods, and small frozen or live invertebrates — baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Moina, Daphnia, and grindal worms are generally taken readily. Foods must be small enough to fit the fish's small mouth. Floating or slow-sinking foods are preferred given the species' top-zone orientation.

goodWith: corydoras and other small bottom-dwelling catfish
small tetras and other peaceful characids
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma, Microgeophagus) as dither fish
other pencilfish species kept in separate schools (including Beckford's Pencilfish)
avoidWith: large or boisterous tankmates that may outcompete or intimidate
dwarf shrimp (cherry shrimp, neocaridina) — likely to be predated

imageCandidates.notes: Fajoe; 4399x2932. Sharp group of several fish showing the diagnostic dark diagonal lateral band extending into the lower caudal lobe (diptail), reticulated scales, and reddish snout tip; group shot best represents this schooling species.
SOK; 3401x3155. Striking, tack-sharp single specimen in oblique head-up posture; vibrant silvery flank with the dark band and dark diptail clearly visible.
Cobaltdrew; 4032x3024. Whole school in a planted blackwater biotope showing the characteristic 45-degree oblique posture; slightly soft, kept as context/variety extra.

summary: The Hockeystick Pencilfish (Nannostomus eques) is a small Amazonian schooling fish best known for its distinctive oblique 45-degree swimming posture. Peaceful and a top-water dweller, it is generally recommended for aquarists with some soft-water experience.
careNotes:
The most distinctive trait of this species is its permanent head-up oblique swimming posture — roughly 45 degrees from horizontal — which is entirely normal and not a sign of disease or distress. This angled posture (responsible for the common names 'Hockeystick' and 'Rocket') distinguishes it from nearly all other pencilfish in the genus, including its congener Beckford's Pencilfish (Nannostomus beckfordi), which holds a more horizontal position. New keepers are commonly advised to confirm this behavior is expected before assuming illness.

This species spends the overwhelming majority of its time in the upper water column, near or just below the surface. Floating plants and overhanging vegetation are strongly recommended — they provide cover, diffuse bright light, and appear to reduce stress-related behavior. Tannin-staining the water with Indian almond (catappa) leaves or driftwood tends to be well-received and has been reported to intensify coloration.

Smaller groups have been known to result in skittishness and uneven male rivalry. Males will display and occasionally spar in typical pencilfish fashion — this is normal at adequate group sizes and rarely causes injury.

Like Beckford's Pencilfish, this species exhibits a nocturnal color shift: a vertical dark barring pattern appears at night and fades by day. This is a well-documented, normal behavior and is not a disease indicator.

A tight-fitting lid is generally advisable for pencilfish, as surface-dwelling species have been known to jump when startled. Gentle filtration — a sponge filter is often recommended — is preferred to avoid strong current, which the species is not adapted to in the wild.

pH values between 5.0 and 7.0 are broadly agreed upon as the safe working range for captive maintenance.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is considered uncommon and is generally rated as moderately difficult. The species deposits eggs on the undersides of plant leaves. No parental care is provided and adults will consume eggs if not removed. A densely planted, species-only breeding tank with very soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5) and subdued light is commonly described as the most favorable setup. Fry require infusoria or very fine powdered foods as first foods; baby brine shrimp can be introduced once fry are large enough to take them.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Pencilfish care guide covering N. eques specifically. Confirmed common names (Hockeystick, Brown, Diptail Pencilfish), diagonal swimming posture, swim zone (top), temperature (24-27°C), pH (6.5-7.5), min group size (6), diet (baby brine, daphnia, cyclops, crushed flake), compatibility. Tank size 10 gal minimum noted.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (Characiformes / Lebiasinidae), native range (central and upper Amazon, Orinoco, Guyana rivers), adult size (25-35mm body length), temperature (22-28°C), pH (4.5-7.5), hardness (18-179 ppm ~ 1-10 dGH), min group size (10+), oblique swimming posture, nocturnal barring pattern, and egg-laying on plant leaf undersides. Recommended min tank base 60x30cm (~54L).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Characiformes / Lebiasinidae), max size 5.0cm TL, temperature 23-28°C, pH 5.0-6.0, hardness dH 5-12, native range (central and upper Amazon, Brazil, Peru, Guyana). FishBase pH range (5.0-6.0) is narrower than trade sources — final pH range set to 5.0-7.0, reflecting the broader but well-supported captive tolerance reported by SeriouslyFish and Aquarium Co-Op.

fish.breedingNotes: The Hockeystick Pencilfish deposits eggs on the undersides of plant leaves and provides no parental care; adults will consume eggs if left in the tank. Removing adults after spawning, or using dense moss to shield the eggs, tends to improve fry survival. Fry require infusoria or very fine powdered foods as their first foods.
